g.data-narration: Welcome to Operating System Operations. We explore how OS manages hardware, provides services, and protects the system.
g.text: Dual-Mode Operation, System Calls, Interrupts & Resource Management

g.data-narration: An OS is system software that manages hardware and software resources, providing services to computer programs.
Modern OS uses dual-mode operation to prevent user programs from interfering with critical system operations.
g.text: Protects system integrity by restricting user programs from executing
privileged instructions. Prevents accidental or malicious damage.

g.data-narration: Dual-mode operation consists of User Mode with limited privileges and Kernel Mode with full system privileges.
Transitions between modes occur through system calls or interrupts, carefully controlled by the operating system.
System calls provide the interface between user programs and the OS, requesting services like file operations and process control.
g.text: System Calls: Bridge to OS Services
The interface between running programs and operating system.
User programs request services through system calls.

g.data-narration: Five main types: Process Control, File Management, Device Management, Information Maintenance, and Communication.
Seven-step process: prepare parameters, execute trap, switch to kernel, identify service, execute, return results, resume.
g.text: Program executes trap instruction CPU switches to kernel mode OS validates
system call number Executes service Returns control with results Program resumes

g.data-narration: Interrupts are signals requiring immediate attention. Hardware interrupts come from devices; software interrupts (traps) come from programs.
Interrupt handling: save CPU state, identify interrupt source, execute handler, restore state, resume execution.
Timers prevent CPU monopolization. When timer expires, interrupt generates allowing OS to regain control for preemptive scheduling.
Process management includes creation (allocating memory, initializing PCB), termination (releasing resources), and context switching.
Context switching saves current process state to PCB, restores next process state, updates registers, then resumes execution.
OS manages CPU, memory, I/O, and files. It ensures efficiency, fairness, isolation, and protection of system resources.
Web server example shows OS coordinating: network interrupt creates process, system calls handle file I/O, memory buffers, network transmission.
g.text: Client request arrives OS creates process/thread System calls read files
OS manages memory buffers Network system calls Process terminates after response

g.data-narration: Key points: dual-mode protects system, system calls bridge user-kernel, interrupts enable async events, OS manages all resources efficiently.
g.text: Dual-Mode Operation | System Calls | Interrupts | Process and Memory Management | Resource Management
